title: Which Revenue Stream Is Actually Carrying Your Veterinary Practice?
linkedin:
A full appointment book doesn't tell you which revenue line is actually carrying your practice.

For most companion animal vet practices, that answer is rarely the one the schedule makes look busiest.

General consultations drive daily flow and client relationships. But the margin per consultation is constrained by time. In a practice where the principal runs most of the general caseload, the return on their highest-skill hours in the consult room is often the lowest activity in the business.

Surgical work tells a different story. Revenue per clinical hour during a surgical list frequently exceeds the consultation room by a significant margin. Diagnostic equipment, once running, generates recurring income at low incremental cost. Dispensing has been compressed in some categories by online channels, but the stronger product lines have held.

When June closes, a full year of billing data allows a ranked view of which activity types are generating the strongest contribution, which have drifted, and where pricing or caseload decisions should shift. That picture is rarely what most principals expect.

There is also a valuation dimension worth naming. A practice where surgical work depends entirely on the principal carries a discount at exit of one to two times normalised annual profit. It is a structural fact that a few years of deliberate team development can change.

If this year's clinical activity has been strong but the margin picture behind it has not been mapped, EOFY is the natural moment to build it.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#FractionalCFO #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness

Do you know which revenue line is actually carrying your vet practice into EOFY?

For most principals, it's not the one filling the appointment book. Surgical work generates far more revenue per clinical hour than general consultations, yet many practices have the principal running wellness consults all day.

If this year's billing has been strong but you haven't looked at the margin behind each stream, EOFY is the moment to do it.

google:
New on the ProfitPulse blog: Which Revenue Stream Is Actually Carrying Your Veterinary Practice?

For companion animal vet practices in Queensland, NSW, and Victoria, EOFY brings the natural moment to ask a question many principals have not yet looked at clearly: which revenue line is actually driving the margin?

A busy practice with a full appointment book and a stocked dispensary can look financially sound at a glance. The detailed picture is usually more layered. Surgical work generates significantly more revenue per clinical hour than general consultations. Diagnostic equipment, once deployed and running, produces recurring income at attractive margins. Dispensing varies by product line, with some categories compressed by online channels and others holding firm.

There is also a valuation dimension that matters for principals thinking about succession or exit in the next three to five years. A practice where surgical work depends entirely on the owner carries a measurable discount at sale. Two to three years of deliberate team development, building surgical capacity across more than one clinician, can close that gap considerably.

The full piece maps the economics of each revenue stream and explains what the EOFY billing picture should surface. If this year's clinical activity has been strong but the margin behind it has not been reviewed by service line, that analysis is worth doing before the accounts are filed away.

Read the full article here: https://profit-pulse.com.au/which-revenue-stream-is-actually-carrying-your-veterinary-practice
